IEEE TAES · Vol. 59 · No. 6 · 2023 DOI 10.1109/TAES.2023.3282192

Effect of Target Motion on Equivalent Transmit Beamforming for the Frequency Diverse Array

Abeer Nasir Chaudhry · Hasan Saeed Mir · Kainam Thomas Wong

3 institutions across
Pakistan, UAE, China
1st analytical generalization
of ETB to moving targets
T3dB new tractable measure
for ETB design trade-offs
Read on IEEE Xplore → Walkthrough below ↓
01

Abstract in plain terms

A traditional phased array can steer a beam in a direction, but it cannot discriminate between a target sitting at 50 km and one at 150 km along that same bearing. Both fall inside the beam.

The Frequency Diverse Array (FDA), paired with a receiver scheme called Equivalent Transmit Beamforming (ETB), changes that. By emitting a slightly different frequency from each antenna element, the array carves out a beam that is selective in both range and angle, a spotlight, not a searchlight.

The catch: the foundational ETB literature assumed the target was standing still. In real radar, targets move, sometimes at 1 km/s or faster. This paper extends the theory to the moving-target case, derives the time-varying array factor analytically, and shows that within just 500 µs a fast target can drift from the mainbeam peak straight into a null.

Field Statistical signal processing · Radar
Method Closed-form derivation · Beampattern analysis · Numerical study
Outcome Generalized ETB array factor · T3dB design metric · Trade-off curves
Applications Range-selective surveillance · Decoy nulling · Threshold-crossing alerts
Funding AUS Grant FRG21-M-E80 · NSFC #62071018
02

Why range-selective beamforming matters

01

Reduce the radar's compute load

Instead of processing every range bin out to the horizon, the radar can scan only the subranges that are tactically interesting, saving cycles for tracking and discrimination.

02

Detect threshold crossings

Early-warning systems need to know when a target crosses a specific range line. A spotlight on that line, not the whole bearing, gives a cleaner trigger with fewer false positives.

03

Null passive jammers

Ship-towed radar decoys and chaff sit at known offsets from the protected platform. A beampattern that nulls a specific subrange, not just a direction, can suppress them without losing the main target.

03

FDA + ETB the geometry

The transmitter is a uniform linear array of $M$ antennas with spacing $d$, each emitting a tone at $f_m = f_0 + (m-1)\Delta_f$. The receiver collocates with element 1 and separates the $M$ echoes by frequency, weighting and summing them to form the ETB output. The target sits at polar coordinates $(R_1, \theta_1)$ and moves with velocity $\mathbf{v}$ at heading $\theta_v$.

04

The contribution in three equations

[1]
$$\AF^{(\mathbf{w})}(R, \theta; t) \;=\; \sum_{m=1}^{M} w_m^{*} \, e^{\, j \Delta_m^{(\phi)}(t)}$$

The generalized ETB array factor. The original work by Xu et al. (2019) stopped at the stationary target case; here the phase $\Delta_m^{(\phi)}(t)$ carries an explicit dependence on time $t$ through the target's velocity. The complex weights $w_m$ implement spatial matched filtering toward a steered location $(R_s, \theta_s)$.

[2]
$$u \;=\; v\,t\!\left[\frac{d}{R}\sin(\theta_v) - 2\,\frac{\Delta_f}{f_0}\cos(\theta - \theta_v)\right] + R\!\left[\frac{d}{R}\sin(\theta) - 2\,\frac{\Delta_f}{f_0}\right]$$

The newly defined ETB range-direction cosine. This is the paper's central coordinate: it collapses range, angle, velocity, and time into a single scalar on which the array factor exhibits translational invariance, $\mathrm{AF}^{(\mathbf{w}=\mathbf{w}_s)}(u) = \mathrm{AF}^{(\mathbf{w}=\mathbf{1})}(u - u_s)$, a direct generalization of the classical phased-array direction cosine.

[3]
$$\T3dB \;=\; \frac{1.4\, c}{\pi M v \left| \dfrac{f_0\, d\, \sin(\theta_v)}{R} - 2\,\Delta_f\cos(\theta - \theta_v)\right|}$$

The mobile-target dwell time inside the 3-dB beamwidth. This is the headline design equation: for given $(M, \Delta_f, f_0, d)$, it tells the system architect how long a target moving at velocity $v$ stays detectable in the mainlobe before drifting into a sidelobe or null. Worst-case form: $T_{3\mathrm{dB}}^{\text{wc}} = \dfrac{1.4 c}{\pi M v\!\left(\frac{f_0 d}{R} + 2\Delta_f\right)}$.

05

What the beampattern actually does

Consider a high-resolution radar with $f_0 = 10\,\mathrm{GHz}$, $M = 64$, $\Delta_f = 5\,\mathrm{MHz}$, and a target at $R_s = 100\,\mathrm{km}$, $\theta_s = 40°$ moving at $1000\,\mathrm{m/s}$. The four-panel figure below, reproduced from the paper, shows the ETB beampattern at three time slices $(t = 0, 250, 500\,\mu s)$ together with the decay of echo strength at the target's initial location.

06

The design tension resolution vs. dwell

↗

Want sharp resolution?

Larger $M$, larger $\Delta_f$. The mainlobe narrows in both range and angle. Targets are localized precisely. Interference is rejected. But, the moving target slips out of the narrow beam in microseconds.

↓ T3dB
vs.
↔

Want long dwell on a fast target?

Smaller $M$, smaller $\Delta_f$. The beam is wide enough that the target stays within the mainlobe for hundreds of microseconds. But, range and angular resolution suffer, and clutter pours in alongside.

↑ T3dB

The paper resolves this through Eq. [3], the worst-case $T_{3\mathrm{dB}}$ formula, which lets the radar designer pick $(M, \Delta_f)$ such that any target moving below a chosen maximum velocity is guaranteed to stay inside the 3-dB beamwidth for a target dwell window. As a worked example: for a 64-element array at $f_0 = 10\,\mathrm{GHz}$, $\Delta_f \approx 2\,\mathrm{MHz}$ keeps a $1000\,\mathrm{m/s}$ target detectable for at least $500\,\mu s$.

07

Challenges along the way

Mathematical

The closed form was not obvious

The full round-trip phase contains a $v t \, d / R^2$ cross-term that, naively, breaks translational invariance. Showing this term vanishes under the $d \ll R$ far-field assumption required careful linearization and small-angle book-keeping.

Conceptual

The "direction cosine" needed re-thinking

The classical phased-array direction cosine sin(θ) had to be replaced by a quantity coupling $R, \theta, \theta_v, v$, and $t$. Identifying the right scalar, one on which the array factor remained translation-invariant, was the unlock for the entire analysis.

Practical

Translating theory into a design metric

A beautiful array factor is academic without a knob the system designer can actually turn. $T_{3\mathrm{dB}}$ was deliberately constructed to be solvable in closed form for the worst-case geometry, giving an explicit $(M, \Delta_f, v)$ trade-off curve.

Physical

Sidelobe height is invariant. surprisingly

A non-obvious result fell out of the Dirichlet-kernel form: the mainlobe-to-sidelobe ratio depends on $M$ alone, not on $\Delta_f$, geometry, or velocity. Useful, but it took a re-derivation to convince ourselves it wasn't an algebra error.

08

Conclusions

A conventional phased array has an array factor that depends only on angle. The FDA-based ETB array factor depends on both angle and range. This paper's central contribution is to show that, with target motion, it depends additionally on time.

  1. Generalized direction cosine. A new coordinate u(R, θ, θv, v, t) on which the array factor is translation-invariant, extending the classical concept from 1-D angle to 5-D angle-range-velocity-time.
  2. Closed-form mainlobe properties. Mainlobe width, sidelobe height, and the fact that the mainlobe-to-sidelobe ratio depends solely on M.
  3. T3dB as a design knob. An analytical metric for how long a moving target stays inside the 3-dB beamwidth, turning a theoretical observation into something a radar architect can actually optimize against.
  4. Worst-case design tables. Numerical surfaces over (v, Δf, M) that let an engineer pick parameters guaranteeing dwell on a fast tactical target.

The result is a self-contained framework that takes ETB from a stationary-target curiosity toward a tool useful for actual moving-target radar problems, the regime in which radar actually operates.

Cite as
A. N. Chaudhry, H. S. Mir, and K. T. Wong, "Effect of Target Motion on Equivalent
Transmit Beamforming for the Frequency Diverse Array," IEEE Transactions on
Aerospace and Electronic Systems, vol. 59, no. 6, pp. 8053 to 8062, Dec. 2023,
doi: 10.1109/TAES.2023.3282192.
09

From the thesis two more contributions, beyond the paper

The IEEE TAES paper above extracts and polishes one chapter of a larger thesis. The full work, Practical Considerations in Frequency Diverse Array Radar Signal Processing (AUS, April 2021, supervisor: Dr. Hasan Saeed Mir), carries two further contributions that build on the same foundation.

I.

Extending ETB to 2D planar array geometries

The IEEE paper deals with a uniform linear array. A ULA can spotlight a particular subrange along a single direction in the far field, but surveillance radars often need to scan in two directional dimensions: azimuth and elevation. Chapter 5 of the thesis derives novel array factors for three canonical 2D geometries that maintain the spatial stationarity of the spotlight while adding the second directional dimension.

What changes from the ULA case

The array manifold becomes 3D-dependent on range, azimuth $\phi$, and elevation $\theta$. The translational invariance proven in the ULA case extends but now requires careful index handling along both array axes. Closed-form ETB array factors are derived for each geometry, and a comparison reveals a clean trade-off:

URA
Highest peak-to-sidelobe ratio

Sharpest 2D mainlobe of the three. Element count NM grows fast.

Cost Cannot span full 360° in azimuth.
LSA
Fewest elements needed

Only N + M − 1 elements vs NM for the URA. Beampattern is a slightly degraded URA pattern.

Best when Element count is the binding constraint.
UCA
Full 360° azimuth coverage

The only one of the three that scans the full circle in azimuth, with better spatial resolution.

Cost Marginally worse peak-to-sidelobe ratio than URA.

The 2D extension is also time-varying

The IEEE paper proved that target motion makes the 1D ULA beampattern time-dependent. The same effect carries over to the 2D geometries. The animations below show the LSA ETB array factor evolving over time for a moving target — the spotlight smears and migrates exactly as the 1D theory predicts, but now in two directional dimensions plus range. This is what motivates the planar mutual-coupling analysis in the next contribution: only after the 2D model is in hand can the realistic predistortion problem be posed cleanly.

II.

Mutual coupling compensation via predistortion

In real arrays, neighbouring antenna elements leak energy into each other. This mutual coupling distorts the beampattern enough that the clean ETB analysis stops being predictive — directivity collapses, sidelobes spike, the spotlight smears. Chapter 6 of the thesis develops a joint estimation algorithm for the mutual coupling coefficients and target parameters, then uses the estimated coupling matrix $\hat{\mathbf{C}}$ to predistort the transmitted signal.

The compensation works because $\hat{\mathbf{C}}^{-1} \mathbf{C} \mathbf{s}(t) \approx \mathbf{I}\,\mathbf{s}(t) = \mathbf{s}(t)$, recovering the ideal radiation characteristics. A symmetric receive-side compensation requires LNA, downconverter, and ADC behind every element, but importantly, no per-element bandpass filter bank or oscillator is needed. The net result: the ETB beampattern recovers its intended directivity and peak-to-sidelobe ratio under realistic mutual-coupling conditions.

Thesis defended at the American University of Sharjah in April 2021. Supervisor: Dr. Hasan Saeed Mir. Committee: Dr. Lutfi Albasha, Dr. Rana Ahmed.
